title: NIST SP 800-171 Revision 3 - Master Assessment (17 Families)

items.text: Limit system access to authorized users, processes acting on behalf of authorized users, and authorized devices.
Limit system access to the types of transactions and functions that authorized users are permitted to execute.
Control the flow of CUI in accordance with approved authorizations.
Separate the duties of individuals to reduce the risk of malevolent activity without collusion.
Employ the principle of least privilege, including for specific security functions and privileged accounts.
Use non-privileged accounts or roles when accessing non-security functions.
Prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions and audit the execution of such functions.
Limit unsuccessful logon attempts.
Provide privacy and security notices consistent with applicable CUI rules.
Use session lock with pattern-hiding displays to prevent access after a period of inactivity.
Terminate a network session after a defined period of inactivity.
Monitor and control remote access sessions.
Employ cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of remote access sessions.
Route remote access through managed access control points.
Authorize remote execution of privileged commands and remote access to security-relevant information.
Authorize wireless access prior to allowing such connections.
Protect wireless access using authentication and encryption.
Control the connection of mobile devices.
Encrypt CUI on mobile devices and mobile computing platforms.
Verify and control/limit connections to and use of external systems.
Limit the use of organizational portable storage devices on external systems.
Control the posting or processing of CUI on publicly accessible systems.
Ensure managers, systems administrators, and users are made aware of security risks and policies.
Ensure personnel are trained to carry out their assigned information security-related duties.
Provide awareness training on recognizing and reporting indicators of insider threat.
Create and retain system audit logs and records to the extent needed to enable monitoring and investigation.
Ensure that the actions of individual system users can be uniquely traced to those users.
Review and update logged events.
Alert in the event of an audit logging process failure.
Correlate audit record review, analysis, and reporting processes for investigation and response.
Provide a system-wide time source that is used to generate time stamps for audit records.
Protect audit information and audit tools from unauthorized access, modification, and deletion.
Limit management of audit logging functionality to a subset of privileged users.
Report failures in the audit logging process to designated personnel.
Establish and maintain baseline configurations and inventories of system components.
Establish, implement, and manage security configuration settings for IT products.
Track, review, approve, and log changes to systems.
Analyze the security impact of changes prior to implementation.
Define, document, and enforce physical and logical access restrictions associated with changes.
Configure systems to provide only essential capabilities.
Restrict, disable, or prevent the use of nonessential programs, functions, ports, protocols, and services.
Apply deny-by-exception (blocklisting) or permit-by-exception (allowlisting) for software.
Control and monitor user-installed software.
Identify and authenticate system users, processes acting on behalf of users, and devices.
Authenticate identities as a prerequisite to allowing access.
Use multifactor authentication for local and network access to privileged and non-privileged accounts.
Employ unique identifiers for each user.
Prevent reuse of identifiers for a defined period.
Disable identifiers after a defined period of inactivity.
Enforce a minimum password complexity and change of characters for new passwords.
Prohibit password reuse for a defined number of generations.
Allow temporary password use but require an immediate change to a permanent password.
Store and transmit only cryptographically protected passwords.
Obscure feedback of authentication information during the logon process.
Establish an operational incident-handling capability (preparation, detection, analysis, containment, recovery).
Track, document, and report incidents to designated officials and authorities.
Test the incident response capability.
Perform, document, and schedule system maintenance.
Provide controls on the tools, techniques, and personnel used to conduct system maintenance.
Sanitize equipment of all CUI before removal for off-site maintenance.
Check media containing diagnostic/test programs for malicious code before use.
Require MFA to establish nonlocal maintenance sessions and terminate sessions when complete.
Supervise the maintenance activities of personnel without required access authorizations.
Protect (identify, physically control, and securely store) system media containing CUI.
Limit access to CUI on system media to authorized users.
Sanitize or destroy system media containing CUI before disposal or release for reuse.
Mark media with necessary CUI markings and distribution limitations.
Control and maintain accountability for CUI media during transport outside controlled areas.
Implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect CUI on digital media during transport.
Control the use of removable media on organizational systems.
Prohibit the use of portable storage devices that have no identifiable owner.
Protect the confidentiality of backup CUI at storage locations.
Screen individuals prior to authorizing access to systems containing CUI.
Ensure systems containing CUI are protected during and after personnel actions like terminations/transfers.
Limit physical access to organizational systems and equipment to authorized individuals.
Protect the physical plant and support infrastructure for organizational systems.
Escort visitors and monitor visitor activity.
Maintain audit logs of physical access.
Control and manage physical access devices (keys, badges, cards).
Enforce physical access authorizations at entry and exit points.
Periodically assess the risk to organizational operations, assets, and individuals.
Scan for system vulnerabilities periodically and when new vulnerabilities are identified.
Remediate vulnerabilities in accordance with risk assessments.
Periodically assess the security controls in systems to determine effectiveness.
Develop and implement plans of action (POA&M) to correct deficiencies and reduce vulnerabilities.
Monitor security controls on an ongoing basis.
Develop, document, and periodically update system security plans (SSP).
Monitor, control, and protect communications at external and key internal system boundaries.
Employ architectural designs, software development techniques, and engineering principles that promote security.
Separate user functionality from system management functionality.
Prevent unauthorized/unintended information transfer via shared system resources.
Separate publicly accessible system components from internal networks.
Deny network communications traffic by default and allow by exception.
Prevent remote devices from simultaneous non-remote and external network communication (Split Tunneling).
Implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality and integrity of CUI during transmission.
Terminate a communications session at the end of the session or after a period of inactivity.
Establish and manage cryptographic keys for cryptography employed in the system.
Employ FIPS-validated cryptography when used to protect the confidentiality of CUI.
Prohibit remote activation of collaborative computing devices and provide an explicit indication of use.
Control and monitor the use of mobile code.
Control and monitor the use of VoIP technologies.
Protect the authenticity of communications sessions.
Protect the confidentiality of CUI at rest.
Identify, report, and remediate system flaws in a timely manner.
Provide protection from malicious code at system entry and exit points.
Monitor the system to detect indicators of potential attacks and unauthorized access.
Detect unauthorized changes to software, firmware, and information.
Employ spam protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points.
Receive, analyze, and respond to security alerts, advisories, and directives.
Verify the integrity of the system.
Develop, document, and disseminate security planning policies and procedures.
Maintain a System Security Plan (SSP) that defines boundaries and safeguards.
Establish and acknowledge Rules of Behavior prior to granting system access.
Integrate security requirements and engineering principles into the SDLC.
Identify and replace unsupported system components (End-of-Life).
Ensure and monitor external service providers' compliance with security requirements.
Develop and document a Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM) plan.
Use acquisition strategies and procurement methods to protect against supply chain risks.
Identify and address supply chain deficiencies and limit disruption harm.
